title: Regular Exercise May Help Protect Against Severe COVID-19

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
Regular Exercise May Help Protect Against Severe COVID-19
More exercise means less risk of developing severe COVID-19, according to a study of physical activity
and coronavirus hospitalizations. The study, which involved 48,440 adults from California who
developed COVID-19 during the period of Jan 1st 2020 to Oct 21st 2020, found that those who had
been the most active before falling ill were 70% less likely to be hospitalized.
This study was conducted at Kaiser Permanente Southern California (KPSC), an integrated healthcare
system that serves approximately 4.7 million residents in Southern California at 15 medical centers.
Subjects of the study have to be enrolled at KPSC for at least 6 months prior to their COVID-19
diagnosis, and they are required to have at least three outpatient visits for the trained medical
assistants or nurses to ask their typical exercise habits over the previous 2 months.
Of the 48,440 subjects in the study, 6,984 were classified as "inactive" (exercise < 10 mins/week),
3,118 as "most active" (exercise > 150 mins/week) and 38,338 as "some activity" (exercise 10 to 150
mins/week). It’s found that 732 of the inactive were hospitalized, compared with 99 from the most
active group.
A 10-minute walk might be especially beneficial for people awaiting their first vaccine, says Dr. Robert
Sallis, a family and sports medicine doctor at the Kaiser Permanente Fontana Medical Center, who led
the study. "I would never suggest that someone who does regular exercise should consider not getting
the vaccine. But until they can get it, I think regular exercise is the most important thing they can do
to lessen their risk. And doing regular exercise will likely be protective against any new variants, or the
next new virus out there." (a) Is the above study a (i) randomized experiment, (ii) case-control study, (iii)
prospective study or (iv) retrospective study? Explain

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text: The above study is a prospective study, as it follows a group of individuals over a period of time and observes their physical activity levels and COVID-19 hospitalizations.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
For each of the 3 options you did not choose in part (a), provide one reason
on why it’s not preferred. (That is, if you chose (i) in part (a), you should
provide one reason for why (ii) is not preferred, (iii) is not preferred and (iv)
is not preferred).

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
(ii) A case-control study would not be preferred in this scenario, as it involves identifying a group of individuals who have the disease (cases) and comparing them to a group without the disease (controls). However, in this study, all participants had COVID-19, and the researchers were interested in how their physical activity levels affected their likelihood of hospitalization.

(iii) A retrospective study would not be preferred, as it involves looking back at past data and medical records. In this case, such a study design would not be ideal, as the researchers would not have control over the information collected and have less ability to verify the accuracy of the data.

(iv) A randomized experiment would not be preferred in this case, as it would be impractical and unethical to randomly assign individuals to different levels of physical activity during a pandemic. Additionally, it would not be possible to blind participants or researchers to the level of physical activity, as the participants themselves would need to report their activity levels.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
(c) Describe how the above study can be performed through a case-control
study by including the following:
(i) How the subjects were chosen? Are there any criteria/restrictions on
the subjects?
(ii) How the two groups are formed?
Regular Exercise May Help Protect Against Severe COVID-19
More exercise means less risk of developing severe COVID-19, according to a study of physical activity
and coronavirus hospitalizations. The study, which involved 48,440 adults from California who
developed COVID-19 during the period of Jan 1st 2020 to Oct 21st 2020, found that those who had
been the most active before falling ill were 70% less likely to be hospitalized.
This study was conducted at Kaiser Permanente Southern California (KPSC), an integrated healthcare
system that serves approximately 4.7 million residents in Southern California at 15 medical centers.
Subjects of the study have to be enrolled at KPSC for at least 6 months prior to their COVID-19
diagnosis, and they are required to have at least three outpatient visits for the trained medical
assistants or nurses to ask their typical exercise habits over the previous 2 months.
Of the 48,440 subjects in the study, 6,984 were classified as "inactive" (exercise < 10 mins/week),
3,118 as "most active" (exercise > 150 mins/week) and 38,338 as "some activity" (exercise 10 to 150
mins/week). It’s found that 732 of the inactive were hospitalized, compared with 99 from the most
active group.
A 10-minute walk might be especially beneficial for people awaiting their first vaccine, says Dr. Robert
Sallis, a family and sports medicine doctor at the Kaiser Permanente Fontana Medical Center, who led

to lessen their risk. And doing regular exercise will likely be protective against any new variants, or the
next new virus out there."
(iii) How the explanatory variable is assigned to (or observed from) the
two groups?
(iv) What's the key measurement/statistic you would like to compare
between the two groups?

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
(i) In a case-control study, the subjects would be chosen based on whether they were hospitalized with severe COVID-19 (cases) or not (controls). To limit the influence of other factors, the subjects should be matched in terms of age, gender, and other relevant factors.

(ii) The two groups would be formed based on whether the subjects were hospitalized with severe COVID-19 or not. Cases would be chosen from the group of individuals who were hospitalized for COVID-19 during the study period, while controls would be chosen from the group of individuals who were not hospitalized for COVID-19 during the study period.

(iii) The explanatory variable (physical activity level) would be assigned to (or observed from) the two groups based on each subject's self-reported physical activity level in the two months prior to their COVID-19 diagnosis. The physical activity level of cases (hospitalized individuals) would be compared to the physical activity level of controls (non-hospitalized individuals).

(iv) The key measurement/statistic that would be calculated in this case-control study is the odds ratio (OR) of hospitalization for those who were considered "most active" (exercise > 150 mins/week) compared to those who were considered "inactive" (exercise < 10 mins/week), adjusted for potential confounding factors.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
(d) Comment on reliability of the news based on each of the 3 critical
components below. Which one do you think is the least reliable? Explain.
(i) Who are the individuals (or objects) of the study, and how they were
selected.
(ii) Differences in groups being compared, in addition to the factor of
interest
(iii) The Extent/size of any claimed effects or differences

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
(i) The news provides clear information on the individuals used in the study, which were adults from California who developed COVID-19 between January 1st, 2020, and October 21st, 2020, through an integrated healthcare system. The study's subjects also had to be enrolled at KPSC for at least six months before their COVID-19 diagnosis, and they were required to have at least three outpatient visits to determine their physical activity level.

(ii) The news reports differences in the physical activity levels of the groups being compared, as well as the factor of interest, i.e., the likelihood of being hospitalized with COVID-19. The groups were classified as "inactive" (exercise < 10 mins/week), "some activity" (exercise 10 to 150 mins/week), and "most active" (exercise > 150 mins/week).

(iii) The news reports a 70% decrease in the likelihood of being hospitalized with COVID-19 for those who were most active (exercise > 150 mins/week) compared to those who were inactive (exercise < 10 mins/week). The study's size was also significant, covering 48,440 individuals.

Based on the critical components above, the least reliable component of the news is the extent/size of the claimed effects. While the study is significant, covering over 48,000 individuals, the 70% decrease in the likelihood of hospitalization may not be a reliable estimate of the true effect of physical activity on COVID-19 hospitalization risk. Other factors may be at play, and the study cannot establish a causal relationship between physical activity and hospitalization.
